FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, ABCD 5.0 release, n=4,641 ages 9-12, 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) on ABCD longitudinal data across baseline, Year 1, and Year 2 follow-up; mediation analyses also performed.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions; no evidence of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. The SMA measure is defined as 'total number of hours spent on non-school-related SMA on a typical day,' a composite that 'mainly included time spent watching TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting on electronic devices, visiting social networking sites, and video-chatting' (Methods, SMA section). No per-modality breakout (e.g., separate coefficient for texting, SNS, or video chat) appears in main tables, Figs 1-3, or supplementary material. Step 2: The composite bundles TV/movies, video games, and phone/SM items without separate breakout of non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, gaming). Composite fails Step 2 because it includes TV and gaming without isolation.

notes: SMA composite explicitly includes TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, social networking sites, and video-chatting. No per-modality estimates appear anywhere in the paper. Composite fails IC2 Step 2 because TV and gaming are bundled with phone/SM items without separate breakout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, ABCD Release 4.0 (September 2021), N=10,828 youth aged 9-11 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal study using multilevel logistic regression (SAS GLIMMIX) examining bidirectional associations between sleep duration, screen time, and internalizing symptoms over one year.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in Journal of Adolescent Health; no evidence of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Supplementary Table A7 reports exploratory associations between individual screen time categories on weekends and withdrawn/depressed symptoms, with separate estimates for each screen type including texting, video chatting, and social networking sites (phone/SM-specific variables). These are paired with withdrawn/depressed symptoms (internalizing outcome = individual-trait-level). Additionally, the main screen time variable is a sum of 6 types including texting, video chatting, and social networking sites; although the composite also includes TV/streaming and gaming, the paper reports per-category estimates in Table A7. At minimum, the texting, video chat, and SNS items from Table A7 constitute phone/SM-specific estimates paired with an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% CI reported for screen time associations with internalizing symptoms (e.g., Table 3: weekend screen time >2 hrs/day vs. ≤2 hrs/day associated with withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females, adjusted OR 1.6 [95% CI 1.1-2.2], p=.0129).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed using multilevel logistic regression with ORs and 95% CIs; association between screen time and internalizing symptoms reported in Tables 3 and 4 and supplementary tables.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is used as IV (predictor) in the primary analyses (baseline screen time predicting follow-up internalizing symptoms, Table 3), so condition (b) is not satisfied. EC7 does not apply when SM/screen time appears as IV in any analysis.
notes: Screen time measure is total of 6 types (TV/streaming, YouTube, videogames, texting, video chatting, SNS). Individual screen type analyses exist in Table A7 (supplementary). Primary design is longitudinal with screen time as IV predicting internalizing symptoms, and reverse-causality models with internalizing as IV predicting screen time (Table 4). IC2 MET via Table A7 phone/SM-specific per-category estimates.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, ABCD Release 4.0, N=10,544 (isotemporal substitution sample), ages 9-11 at baseline (2017-2018).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis (CoDA) in a one-year longitudinal design; all analyses are original analyses of ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity; no evidence of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The 'socializing' screen time category is defined in the Methods as 'texting + video chatting + using social networking sites' — exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items. This variable has its own separate coefficient in all analyses. Table 2 (isotemporal substitution) reports: replacing 30 min of socializing with 30 min of physical activity associated with lower follow-up BMI z-score in females (β [95% CI] = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.002]) and males (β = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.01]). Table 6 (CoDA) reports: in males, greater proportion of socializing associated with higher BMI z-score (β = 0.05 [0.02, 0.08]). BMI z-score is a biological/physical health individual-trait variable. Step 1 is satisfied; stop.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric β estimates with 95% CI reported for socializing screen time in relation to BMI z-score across Tables 2 and 6 (e.g., females isotemporal: β = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.002]; males CoDA: β = 0.05 [0.02, 0.08]).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses conducted via sex-stratified isotemporal substitution linear regression and CoDA with β coefficients and 95% CIs; association between socializing screen time and BMI z-score quantified.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including socializing) is the IV predicting BMI z-score (outcome) in all analyses. Design is longitudinal but SM/screen is IV, not DV. FT-EC7 requires SM to be the DV in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: Three screen time categories: streaming (TV + YouTube), gaming, and socializing (texting + video chat + SNS). Socializing is exclusively phone/SM items and has its own coefficient in all models. BMI z-score is the outcome. Paper INCLUDed under v7 criteria.
